attributes.stem:
A 48-year-old woman is admitted to hospital for elective total laparoscopic hysterectomy. Her only medical history is of adenomyosis and a high body mass index (BMI) of 41kg/m2. She takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her preoperative oxygen saturation is 98% on room air.

Induction of anaesthesia and endotracheal intubation are performed uneventfully, and she is moved into a standard Trendelenburg position to facilitate the procedure. Shortly after the beginning of the surgery, her oxygen saturation drops to 92% despite an inspired oxygen concentration of 100%. Her endotracheal tube position is confirmed to be correct, and there are normal breath sounds bilaterally. Her other vital signs are all within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best explains this decrease in oxygen saturation?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario demonstrates the common physiological changes that occur in the respiratory system during general anaesthesia. The most significant change is the reduction in functional residual capacity (FRC), which typically decreases by 15-20% following induction of anaesthesia.

Decreased functional residual capacity occurs during anaesthesia due to multiple factors including loss of respiratory muscle tone, displacement of the diaphragm by the pressure of the abdominal organs and supine positioning - this effect is exaggerated even further in Trendelenburg (or head-down) position. This leads to atelectasis formation, particularly in dependent lung regions, causing ventilation-perfusion mismatch and increased shunt which cannot be overcome by increasing the fraction of inspired oxygen. General anaesthesia further impairs the body's protective hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstrictive mechanisms, exaggerating the shunt and worsening arterial hypoxia.

The patient's elevated body mass index would make her particularly susceptible to these changes, as obesity is associated with further reductions in FRC and increased tendency to develop atelectasis under general anaesthesia. Management strategies include positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP), recruitment manoeuvres, and maintaining a more neutral or head-up position during surgery if appropriate.

Bronchospasm would typically present with wheeze on auscultation and increased airway pressures during mechanical ventilation, which are not described in this case.

Increased oxygen consumption alone would not account for this degree of desaturation, particularly with supplemental oxygen. Additionally, oxygen consumption typically decreases during general anaesthesia due to reduced metabolic rate.

Pneumothorax would typically present with unilateral decreased breath sounds and potentially cardiovascular compromise, neither of which are present in this case.

Right mainstem bronchus intubation would present with absent breath sounds on the left side and has been excluded by the finding of bilateral breath sounds.
learningPoints: General anaesthesia causes a significant reduction in functional residual capacity due to loss of respiratory muscle tone and supine positioning.
Ventilation-perfusion mismatch increases during general anaesthesia due to atelectasis formation in dependent lung regions.
General anaesthesia impairs hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, leading to increased shunt.
references: Luca Bigatello, Antonio Pesenti; Respiratory Physiology for the Anesthesiologist. Anesthesiology 2019; 130:1064–1077 doi: https://doi.org/10.1097/ALN.0000000000002666

David JW Knight, Ravi P Mahajan, Patient positioning in anaesthesia, Continuing Education in Anaesthesia Critical Care & Pain, Volume 4, Issue 5, October 2004, Pages 160–163, https://doi.org/10.1093/bjaceaccp/mkh044
